metadata.chiefComplaint: I have severe pain in my upper-right tooth, I feel feverish, weak, and sick, and it has gotten much worse.
metadata.title: Upper-Right Odontogenic Abscess with Cellulitis and Systemic Infection

findings.label: Involved upper-right molar
findings.value: Markedly tender to touch and percussion

findings.label: Dentition
findings.value: Poor dentition with no recent dental care

findings.label: Dental source
findings.value: Periapical abscess of the upper-right molar

scripted.response: Hello... I feel really sick.
My upper-right tooth hurts badly, my cheek is swelling, and I feel feverish, weak, and sick.

triggers: how long has this been going on
scripted.response: It has been getting worse for seven days.
It is the back tooth on the upper-right side.
It is constant now, severe and throbbing, about eight out of ten.

triggers: did hot or cold bother it earlier
scripted.response: Earlier in the week, hot and cold triggered it. Now it hurts constantly.
Yes. Chewing hurts, and now I can barely touch the tooth.
Yes. My right cheek has become more swollen over about the last day.
Yes. I have felt feverish and hot.
I feel feverish, but I do not know my exact temperature.
Yes, I have had chills.
I feel weak, tired, and sick.
I take ibuprofen, 400 milligrams as needed, about every six hours.
I have no known drug allergies, and I am not allergic to penicillin.
No, I am not having trouble breathing.
Yes, I can swallow liquids.
No, my voice has not changed.
No, I am not drooling.
I can open my mouth, but it is uncomfortable.
I have not seen a dentist since I lost Medicaid last year.

patientChecklist.label: Asked what brought the patient in
Asked which tooth and side hurts
Asked about earlier hot or cold provocation
Asked about chewing, touching, or percussion pain
Asked about facial swelling and progression
Asked about ibuprofen or home medication use
Asked about medication or penicillin allergy
Asked targeted breathing, swallowing, voice, drooling, or mouth-opening questions
Asked about fever, chills, weakness, fatigue, or systemic illness
Asked about dental care and Medicaid access
Asked about current pain character and severity
Reviewed available examination or diagnostic results

clinicalChecklist.label: Characterizes earlier thermal symptoms and current constant chewing pain
Elicits fever, chills, weakness, fatigue, swelling, and airway red flags
Reviews structured oral, facial, vital-sign, laboratory, and CT findings
Reviews medication, antibiotics, allergies, and source-control needs
Recognizes systemic odontogenic infection with cellulitis as an emergency

patientFacts.text: The upper-right dental pain has worsened over seven days.
The painful tooth is an upper-right back molar.
The pain is now constant, severe, throbbing, and 8/10.
The current pain is 8/10.
Earlier in the illness, hot and cold triggered the pain; it has now become constant.
Chewing is painful, and the involved tooth is extremely painful to touch.
Tapping the upper-right back tooth causes marked pain.
The right cheek swelling has increased over approximately 24 hours.
Subjective fever, chills, fatigue, and increasing right-cheek swelling developed over approximately 24 hours.
The patient has felt feverish and hot.
The patient feels feverish but does not know an exact temperature.
The patient has had chills.
The patient feels weak, fatigued, and systemically ill.
The patient has enlarged lymph nodes but does not know an exact node location or size.
General health is excellent; the patient has no known medical problems.
The patient takes ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, approximately every six hours.
The patient has no contraindication to ibuprofen, Motrin, or Advil.
The patient has no history of opioid or narcotic use, opioid misuse, or opioid abuse.
The patient has no known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
The patient has no difficulty breathing.
The patient can swallow liquids.
The patient's voice has not changed.
The patient is not drooling.
The patient can open the mouth, although it is uncomfortable.
The patient smokes approximately one-half pack of cigarettes per day.
The patient uses alcohol rarely.
The patient denies illicit-drug use.
The patient has not seen a dentist since losing Medicaid last year.
The patient thinks the teeth are in poor condition and has seen spots that may be untreated cavities, without a dentist-confirmed diagnosis.
Prior antibiotic use for this problem is not established; the patient does not know or recall.
Prior root-canal treatment of the affected tooth is not established; the patient does not know or recall.
Other prior treatment of the affected tooth is not established; the patient does not recall having treatment done on that tooth.
The currently painful upper-right molar is present, so that same tooth was not previously extracted.
Whether the patient has had another tooth extracted is not established; the patient does not know or recall.
The patient does not know the exact measured heart rate unless told by the clinician.
The patient does not independently know whether clinical SIRS criteria are met.

history.onset: Pain started with hot and cold provocation and worsened throughout the week.
history.duration: Seven days of worsening upper-right dental pain; cheek swelling and systemic symptoms increased over approximately 24 hours.
history.pain: Initially hot/cold-provoked, now constant, severe, throbbing, and 8/10 with painful chewing and touch.
history.medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed approximately every six hours.
history.allergies: No known drug allergies and no penicillin allergy.
history.medicalHistory: Otherwise healthy; no known opioid-use history and no ibuprofen contraindication.
history.dentalHistory: Poor dentition, untreated cavities, and no recent dental care since losing Medicaid.
history.socialHistory: Smokes approximately half a pack per day; rare alcohol use; no illicit drug use.

hpiFacts: Seven days of worsening upper-right molar pain.
Earlier hot/cold provocation progressed to constant severe throbbing 8/10 pain.
Chewing and touching the tooth are very painful.
Increasing right cheek swelling over approximately 24 hours.
Fever, chills, fatigue, weakness, and systemic illness are present.
Mouth opening is uncomfortable but possible.
No drooling, voice change, dyspnea, or inability to swallow liquids.
redFlags: Risk of progression despite no current deep-space involvement
examinationFindings: Upper-right gingival erythema, swelling, and tenderness
Marked upper-right molar touch and percussion tenderness
Normal voice, no drooling, and no airway distress
CT-confirmed upper-right periapical abscess with facial cellulitis and no deep-space involvement
expectedQuestions: How has the pain changed over seven days?
Did hot or cold trigger it earlier, and is it constant now?
Does chewing or touching the tooth hurt?
Have you had fever, chills, weakness, or fatigue?
Can you breathe, swallow liquids, speak normally, and open your mouth?
Do you have medication allergies, including penicillin or amoxicillin?
When did you last receive dental care?
supportingInfo.diagnosis: Upper-right odontogenic periapical abscess with facial cellulitis and systemic infection.
managementExpectations: Obtain CBC, BMP, lactate, and CT imaging.
Provide IV fluids, analgesia, and antipyretic treatment.
Start IV ampicillin-sulbactam or a faculty-approved equivalent.
Consult OMFS or an appropriate specialist and consider admission.
Arrange definitive dental treatment and source control within approximately 72 hours.
Explain that antibiotics alone do not definitively treat the tooth.
treatmentExpectations: IV fluids, analgesia, and antipyretic therapy.
Definitive dental source control within approximately 72 hours.
referralExpectations: Consider hospital admission based on the emergency systemic-infection context.
safetyNettingExpectations: Do not treat antibiotics alone as definitive dental care.
Escalate for worsening swelling, swallowing difficulty, breathing difficulty, drooling, voice change, or other deep-space signs.
learningObjectives: Recognize cellulitis and systemic infection in an odontogenic abscess.
Interpret structured laboratory and CT results without conflating viewing with recognition.
Plan emergency IV treatment, consultation, possible admission, and timely source control.

keyFindings: Fever, chills, fatigue, weakness, and facial swelling
CT-confirmed periapical abscess with facial cellulitis and no deep-space involvement
criticalMisses: Failure to recognize systemic infection or emergency severity
Failure to assess airway and deep-space red flags
Failure to review laboratory or CT results
Routine discharge with oral antibiotics alone
Failure to arrange specialist input and definitive source control
reportData.idealSummary: A 21-year-old with seven days of worsening upper-right molar pain that progressed from thermal provocation to constant severe throbbing 8/10 pain, with increasing cheek swelling, fever, chills, fatigue, weakness, temperature 38.4°C, heart rate 108, WBC 14.8 with neutrophilia, and CT-confirmed periapical abscess with facial cellulitis but no deep-space involvement, requiring emergency IV treatment, specialist consultation, possible admission, and definitive dental source control.
